ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tawa Chicken Fry. FOODON_03302757 (chicken dish) is the nearest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for tawa-style dry-spiced griddle-fried chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Cooked chicken) is the nearest available concept. No specific tawa preparation concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Tawa Chicken Fry is approximately 250 g, comprising several bone-in or boneless chicken pieces cooked on a tawa with spiced aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry (dry), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tawa Chicken Fry is not listed by name in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN dry chicken curry (cooked, bone-in pieces, spiced with onion, garlic, ginger, chilli, oil) which closely matches the preparation method and ingredient profile. Energy and macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked spiced chicken (NDB 05064) used as a cross-check. Sodium is estimated from typical Indian dry chicken preparation using ICMR-NIN spice and salt additions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Foster-Powell et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked chicken and low-carbohydrate meat dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tawa Chicken Fry. GI is estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of cooked chicken and dry meat dishes as low-GI foods (GI typically 0–20 for protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate meat preparations). Carbohydrate content per 100 g is 3.8 g; GL per 250 g serving = (15 × 9.5 g carb) / 100 = 1.4. This is consistent with peer-reviewed guidance that protein-dominant dishes with minimal starch have negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Tawa Chicken Fry contributes approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing about 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tawa Chicken Fry has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.4 per standard serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium and oil quantity are monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 8 g per 250 g serving and high sodium load are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean chicken cuts, request minimal oil, and limit portion size to manage saturated fat and sodium intake within AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tawa Chicken Fry is a popular North Indian dry chicken preparation in which bone-in or boneless chicken pieces are cooked on a tawa — a heavy flat iron griddle — with a spiced masala of onion, garlic, ginger, green chillies, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and red chilli powder. The dish is characterised by its dry, intensely flavoured coating, slightly charred edges from direct contact with the hot iron surface, and a bold, aromatic spice profile. The texture is firm and juicy on the inside with a lightly caramelised exterior. Tawa Chicken Fry is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants across South Asia and the Indian diaspora in Singapore, Malaysia, and the United Kingdom. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 18.5 g protein and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. The sodium content is moderate to high due to salt and spice blends used in restaurant preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically prepared as halal in Indian Muslim restaurants such as Briyani Hut in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Tawa Chicken Fry?
Is Tawa Chicken Fry good for diabetics?
What is the protein content of Tawa Chicken Fry?
How is Tawa Chicken Fry different from regular fried chicken?
What spices are used in Tawa Chicken Fry?

confidence_reason: Tawa Chicken Fry is not listed by name in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest ICMR-NIN dry chicken curry entry and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central cooked spiced chicken data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match exists in an approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods source for this food category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from ICMR-NIN dry chicken curry and USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken data (NDB 05064) and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed. Sodium is estimated from typical Indian restaurant dry chicken preparation. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Tawa Chicken Fry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal status is inferred from the serving restaurant context (Briyani Hut, an Indian Muslim restaurant); this should be confirmed at source. The dish is served at Briyani Hut – Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 17.90 per portion.
